data.title: Nathan's Famous: Take The (Kind Of Disappointing) Money And Run
data.description:
In one sense, I called it. Nathan's Famous (NATH) is getting acquired by Smithfield Foods (SFD) in a ~$450 million deal. In a pair of articles last year, I argued that a sale seemed likely and even twice called out Smithfield as the most likely acquirer.

But before dislocating my shoulder trying to pat myself on the back, neither call was all that complicated. The fact that Howard Lorber, NATH's largest shareholder, was 76 and had been pushed out of his role at Douglas Ellman (DOUG) was widely known. So was the licensing deal with Smithfield that underpins the overwhelming majority of Nathan's Famous's current profits. I was far from the only investor to see a deal with Smithfield as logical, and indeed a few rallies over the past couple of years (including the steady accumulation up to ~$115 last summer) looked like the market was positioning for a transaction.

And indeed, neither call was all that spectacular — or that lucrative. With an 8.7% gain on Wednesday as of this writing, total returns in NATH since my first piece are ~23%, and negative 9% since I decided to keep my position last summer.

In that context, I obviously see the $102 per share price announced Wednesday as something of a disappointment. But it's an understandable disappointment, particularly with the business showing a bit of softness over the last couple of quarters. And with the deal spread now in the mid-single digits, disappointing or not, the risk/reward clearly is in favor of cutting loose, as I did on Wednesday afternoon.

Really? $102 a Share?

Again, I can see some disappointment in the price here, which in turn might suggest at least the possibility of a higher bid from over the top. Net debt at the end of Q3 was just $17.1 million; reported diluted shares were 4.13 million, but the actual figure seems to be a touch higher (Smithfield is reporting the deal at "approximately" a $450 million enterprise value, while the math above suggests ~$438 million).

As the press release notes, the deal is also at a 12.4x EBITDA multiple (trailing twelve months through calendar Q3 2025), which certainly seems healthy for a hot dog manufacturer. But the business model here is licensing (mostly to Smithfield itself, of course; the meatpacker's John Morell unit actually manufactures the Nathan's hot dog), which suggests a stronger multiple. That's all the more true given that Smithfield estimates it will save about $9 million a year; adding back those synergies (which get EBITDA to just over $45 million), the EBITDA multiple is just under 10x.

It would seem — and, this summer, it did seem — like a buyer might be interested at a bigger multiple. Smithfield itself paid $33 million in royalties to Nathan's Famous over the past four quarters. Cutting that spend alone seems to justify the purchase price, particularly with other businesses to benefit from. Those businesses are less valuable but at least material: licensing revenue ex-Smithfield over the past four quarters is about $4 million, while the Branded Product Program has brought in $5 million and the restaurant business close to $3 million.

It does seem like $102 is a bit of an underpay. There's an argument that licensing alone supports the $450 million price tag (a 12x multiple for a licensor is hardly out of line and, in fact, represents a discount to admittedly larger and likely better players like Domino's (DPZ)), meaning Nathan's perhaps left some value on the table.

And it would then seem to follow that there might be room for another bid. The termination fee here is $10.6 million, hardly enough to deter a buyer that sees value in Nathan's Famous at $110 per share plus.

A White Knight Seems Unlikely

But another buyer seems unlikely for two core reasons. The first is that Nathan's Famous almost certainly executed a lengthy and thorough sales process. Again, Lorber is a veteran of dealmaking (in both the equity markets and real estate). The Financial Times reported almost a year ago that the company was considering a sale; such a leak would make sense if Nathan's Famous was trying to entice potential suitors.

And so the fact that Smithfield is paying $102/share seems to suggest that was simply the best price Nathan's could get. Again, it's a surprise, but surely both strategic buyers and private equity firms at least looked. PE would make quite a bit of sense here: I've speculated in the past that Nathan's now-underlevered balance sheet was likely designed to potentially attract private equity bids. The board paused buybacks and kept the dividend flat despite earnings growth; neither decision makes sense, particularly because a stable licensing profit stream allowed the company to run higher leverage. (Indeed, Nathan's recapitalized itself back in 2015, briefly running at ~5x EBITDA after paying out a large special dividend.)

The second, likely related, issue is that results the last few quarters weren't great. Adjusted EBITDA declined year-over-year in the first half of fiscal 2026, and volume at retail (through Smithfield) plunged 17%. Price increases are part of the issue (they were up 15%), and the 10-Q cites lower promotional activity as a result. Profits in the BPP were down sharply as well, as the cost per pound for hot dogs jumped 16%, outpacing price increases.

Over the past decade-plus, Nathan's Famous has simply been a good business, steadily growing profits and often returning capital to shareholders. But the last two quarters have shown some potential cracks in the business model and do raise the question of what the brand looks like if costs keep rising in a category where consumers can trade down (or simply not pay $12 per pound for hot dogs, even if they're quality hot dogs). One has to wonder if that sense didn't spook some buyers; it seemed in February like there would be plenty of acquirers, but in the end Nathan's seems to have gone with the best offer from the company most likely to do the deal.

Smithfield and Downside

Assuming beef prices eventually settle, this does look like a good deal for Smithfield. Given the $30 million-plus in royalty payments being saved each year, it almost can't be a terrible deal. And it does advance the company's strategy in building out brands and moving up in price points.

As far as NATH goes, the deal spread is relatively narrow. The merger agreement does allow for the payment of up to more dividends; it's possible the merger closes after the second (which should be scheduled for May; Smithfield expects the deal to close in the first half).

Even considering that, the deal spread here does look quite narrow: 2.2% returns over presumably a five-year holding period. Given that six-month Treasuries are yielding 3.6%, that spread (a bit over 5%) looks pretty tight.

And that spread might suggest that buyers see at least a possibility of a second bidder and/or something close to certainty in the deal being closed. As written above, the former seems unlikely; the latter is probably right — with one notable exception. I've written before, mostly tongue-in-cheek, that it's at least possible the sale here becomes a political football.

The idea of a classic American brand — one founded in the city our current president was born and raised in — being sold to a "Chinese" company (Smithfield is still ~88% owned by Hong Kong-based WH Group) could raise some hackles. And given that President Trump has publicly voiced his opinion on various mergers, most notably in the media space, it wouldn't be stunning if the Nathan's deal became a cause célèbre among the online right. That could eventually catch the president's ear.

To be clear, such an outcome is unlikely. But stranger things have happened in this political environment — and the odds of another bid seem similarly slim. And with the spread so narrow, NATH looks like one to leave for the arbitrage professionals.

data.title: Where And Why For Gold And Silver
data.description:
By Brien Lundin

For those who thought 2025 was crazy for gold and silver, 2026 is saying, “Hold my beer.” So far this year, we’ve seen silver reach for the skies and a target of $100 that seemed like a fantasy, or fanatical, just a few weeks ago.

And during that amazing run, gold has been relatively well-behaved, advancing steadily but in an unspectacular manner, at least in comparison to silver.

That’s changed over the past few trading sessions. As you can see from the chart above, gold has accelerated to the upside, seemingly taking aim for the magical $5,000 level in the near future. (This chart makes it seem like gold is down on Wednesday, but thanks to a surge in overnight markets, it’s actually up over $50 from Tuesday.)

Silver is taking a break on Wednesday, and a much-needed one at that. But if you take all of these moves, including the PGMs, base metals, and just about every other commodity, it’s obvious that something’s up.

Let’s take a look at just what that could be.

Who’s Buying at These Levels?

I ran across a post on X by a gold bear this morning, and his point was essentially, “Who do you know who’s actually buying gold at these prices?” Well, if your worldview is limited to U.S. retail investors, then everyone you know is more likely to sell gold than buy right now.

But it’s a great big world, and there are plenty of actors on the global stage who think that gold and silver are bargains at these prices. Of course, we all know about central banks.

They’re not very price sensitive, and apparently are steadily buying on a dollar-cost averaging basis at regular intervals. This means that individual banks are probably buying less in terms of tonnage as the price rises.

The good news for gold bulls is that others are stepping in to pick up whatever slack there is in official purchases. These include investors and institutions in China and India (especially for silver, where the local prices are well above the New York quotes).

And everywhere else around the world, it seems that the “sell America” trade is back on in force, especially given the noise around Greenland. Add in the surge in Japan’s sovereign bond yields, which threatens not only other sovereign yields but every risk market, and you have a powerful recipe for continued gains in monetary metals.

The other metals and commodities are benefiting from strong demand curves running smack-dab into decades of under-investment and strangled supply pipelines. With commodities becoming the latest theme du jour on Wall Street, this trend also seems set to continue. And in turn, this raises the question...

How Much Further Can We Go?

I’ve addressed this question in every presentation I’ve given over the past five years or so, and in those speeches as well as this letter, I’ve featured this slide:

I’ve counted four distinct bull markets since August 15, 1971, when gold began trading as an investable asset, and not the basis of the global financial system. Those four bull markets occurred in the early 1970s, the late 1970s, the 2000s, and today.

In the chart above, I show that gold rose from 5.6 times to 8.2 times, from trough to peak, in each of those previous bull markets. If you count the bottom of this market at around $1,050, then a similar performance this time would put the gold price somewhere between $6,000 and $8,000.

But there’s another possible interpretation...

And that would be if we consider the 1970s as one cohesive bull run, as many analysts do. If that’s the case, then gold rose some 24 times during that period, which would project to a price in the mid-$20,000s at the end of this bull market.

That, in turn, would imply some sort of monetary reset, with gold re-inserted as the foundation for fiat currencies globally. And that’s certainly not out of the question.

It would also imply an interim break in the bull market, and a fairly severe one, like what was experienced in the 1974-1976 timeframe. That’s a possibility that we need to be ready for this time around.

The bottom line is that no one really knows what the future holds for gold, silver, and the rest of the commodity complex. But one thing is clear, and that’s the direction of the trend.

It’s also clear that we want to be on board with this trend, because it’s going a lot higher, albeit with some volatility along the way.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Pinnacle Financial Partners: Attractive With The Synovus Deal Completed
data.description:
Shares of Pinnacle Financial Partners (PNFP) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 15% of their value. Investors suffered whiplash this summer as Pinnacle agreed to buy Synovus after many expected Pinnacle itself to get acquired. The deal was completed on Jan. 2 and is a true “merger of equals” with PNFP holders owning just 52% of the combined company. While there is execution risk, I am bullish on this combination, which drove my “Buy” rating in October, and since then, shares have gained 12%. With updated financials and the merger complete, now is a good time to revisit PNFP.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Pinnacle Financial Partners earned $2.24 per share, which was $0.02 below expectations even as revenue grew 14% to $542 million. Still, earnings were up 18% from last year, reflecting strong balance sheet growth. There was a lot to this quarter since PNFP closed its SNV purchase right after quarter end, so no SNV financials are consolidated into its results but were instead released separately. Including Synovus, Pinnacle has about $117 billion of assets and $96 billion in deposits across its 400 locations in the South.

PNFP reports strong growth; credit costs unlikely to remain elevated

Looking first at legacy PNFP, loans rose 10% from last year to $39.2 billion, and deposits were up 11% to $47.4 billion. As you can see below, PNFP has steadily grown its loan book, and yields are now coming down given Fed rate cuts. Importantly, non-interest-bearing deposits have kept pace, also rising 11% to $9 billion, and it appears these have bottomed as a share of deposits, though I am not expecting any recovery in 2026. Deposit costs ended the quarter down 22 bp to 2.21%, and PNFP has been able to cut rates as quickly as it raised them during the hiking cycle while maintaining strong deposit growth.

PNFP generated $424 million of net interest income, up 13% from last year, and its net interest margin of 3.27% was up 1 bp sequentially. Over the next year, PNFP has over $3.5 billion in loans maturing at a ~5% fixed rate. With current loan rates at 7+%, the company can replace these with higher yields and further boost NIM and interest income.

On credit quality, charge-offs were a bit higher than expected at 28 bps, up 4 bps from last year. However, we also saw nonperforming loans drop to 0.34% of loans, down 5 bps sequentially and 8 bps from last year. During the quarter, Pinnacle set aside $34 million for credit losses. Frankly, it seemed PNFP used Q4 to “clear the deck” and pull forward some charge-offs to provide a clean slate for 2026 with its Synovus merger. Over time, NPAs should drive charge-offs and the steady improvement over the past year should ensure charge-offs run closer to 20bps than 30bps going forward. While higher credit costs drove the miss, I do not expect that to be a recurring theme going forward.

Pinnacle remains well capitalized with a tier 1 common equity (CET1) ratio of 10.9%, up from 10.8% a year ago. The ability to build capital while growing loans in double digits is impressive and speaks to the strong earnings power of the franchise. This leaves Pinnacle with ample financial capacity to support ongoing loan growth, and with the company continuing to aggressively hire revenue-producing bankers (200+ people this year), that remains the clear priority of the firm.

Synovus results were as expected

Now, while Synovus is no longer publicly traded, Pinnacle released its Q4 results as well to give investors a sense of how that business was doing before being acquired. It earned $1.45, which was similar to its Q2 and Q3 results. Importantly, balance sheet momentum continued with loans up 2% sequentially and deposits growing 3% sequentially to $51.2 billion. Given fixed reinvestment and a 16bp drop in deposit costs to 2.07%, SNV’s net interest margin expanded 4bps to 3.45%.

Credit quality also remains solid at legacy Synovus. Net charge-offs were 22 bps, down 4 bps from a year ago, and nonperforming assets were 0.62%, down 11 bps from a year ago. As you can see below, its criticized loans have been steadily falling. Synovus also added $9 million to its credit reserves, which now total $529 million, reflecting ongoing loan growth. At ~5.5x annual charge-offs, SNV is well reserved and already prepared for a modest deterioration in credit quality. The bank remained extremely well capitalized with an 11.3% CET1 ratio; this capital calculation excludes the $628 million loss in accumulated other comprehensive income. Essentially, Q4 transpired as one would expect at Synovus ahead of its acquisition.

2026 outlook is positive for the new PNFP

Now, alongside results, Pinnacle provided 2026 guidance that includes the contribution from Synovus. Pinnacle expects to maintain its strong pace of loan growth with the combined firm growing about 10%. Given PNFP achieved this in 2025, I believe it can do so again, as the loan demand environment should be better this year with less macro uncertainty and lower borrowing costs. Management also expects a broadly similar pace of net charge-offs, consistent with my view that the credit environment should be flattish this year, given slow but positive economic growth.

In terms of integration items related to SNV, management still expects $720 million of merger-related costs through 2027. It continues to target $250 million of cost synergies, though it expects to achieve just 40% of them this year, from 50% previously. Now, when a bank buys another, it marks the assets to market, from an accounting perspective, realizing the loss in AOCI noted above as well as marking loans to the current rate environment. Given changes in rates, it expects -$1.6 billion of marks from -$1.8 billion previously. Because of this, its pro forma capital will be better than first thought. Taken together, I view these developments as offsetting and largely neutral.

The below is a helpful walk of the capital impact of the deal. While the combined entities have an 11.1% CET1 ratio, marking the SNV assets to market immediately moves it to 10%. From there, earnings should offset integration costs, the dividend, and loan growth. Across my coverage of the regional banks over the past few years, I have emphasized that banks' reported CET1 is different from their “pro forma” CET1, including unrealized losses, and investors need to consider both metrics. Essentially, by acquiring SNV, PNFP is “realizing” much of that gap in its regulatory capital position by marking assets to their Day 1 fair value. This makes its regulatory capital ratio more consistent with its true economic position. While the combined regulatory capital ratio is lower, PNFP/SNV’s true financial position is no different. I view ~10% as an appropriate capital level for the combined bank. PNFP plans a $0.50 dividend going forward, for a ~2% dividend yield. Aside from this dividend, I expect PNFP to retain capital to support loan growth and do not view buybacks as likely.

PNFP is a "Buy"

Back in October, I saw PNFP earning $8.75-$9.25 in 2026 on a standalone basis and $9.50-$10 with Synovus, depending on the pace of synergies. We are getting essentially a 12-month contribution from SNV, whereas I previously assumed 10 months, but this benefit is partially offset by a slower realization of synergies. On net, I see Pinnacle earning $9.90-$10.50 this year, excluding one-time merger costs. This leaves shares right around 10x earnings, which I view as attractive for a firm with solid credit quality, clear loan growth momentum, and a good economic capital position. Plus, as synergies are fully realized, that provides a runway for ongoing earnings growth in 2027 past $11, even as I do not assume any of the $130 million in targeted revenue synergies.

Q1 results will likely be very messy on a GAAP basis, given the mark-to-market and subsequent reallocation of legacy SNV’s securities portfolio, but I believe we will quickly see underlying core results show momentum as Pinnacle capitalizes on its leading presence across the Southeast. I expect shares to migrate towards 12x over the next 12-18 months, providing eventual upside towards $130. As such, I remain a "Buy" on PNFP and would accumulate shares here.

data.title: Covenant Logistics Stock Performance Contrasts With Meager Returns On Assets
data.description:
Covenant Logistics (CVLG) is an American truckload carrier specializing in the Dedicated and Expedited sub-segments.

The company's stock has defied the trucking downturn of the past few years with still relatively elevated prices, as CVLG was able to maintain operational profitability.

Although the company might look interesting on this basis, on a fundamental one, it seems relatively unattractive. Its returns on assets are fairly low, even below the company's cost of debt. Even adding back the operations of a profitable JV and using optimistic cycle assumptions, the situation does not improve much. For this reason, I initiate with a Hold on CVLG.

Business intro

Covenant is a trucking carrier, a kind of company of which I have covered several in the past weeks, including J.B. Hunt (JBHT), Schneider National (SNDR), Werner Enterprises (WERN), and Marten Transport (MRTN).

Within trucking, the company focused mainly on the Expedited (34% of FY24's revenues) and Dedicated (~31% of revenues) sub-segments.

Expedited loads are loads that need to be delivered quickly, and therefore are sent in trucks with a team of two drivers, one of whom is resting while the other one drives. This requires a somewhat specialized driver network.

Dedicated, as analyzed in other articles, consists of the management of a fleet of trucks and drivers for specific shippers.

The company's fleet operates mainly in dry-van (70% of trailers), plus some refrigerated (12% of trailers), and then specialty, including a large client in poultry.

In addition to these two, the company operates in Managed Freight (basically brokerage, 25% of revenues in FY24) and in Warehousing (10% of revenues).

Finally, CLVG also owns 49% of a fairly profitable trucking leasing JV that is not consolidated, but that has generated net income to the company of about $20 million per year between FY22 and FY24, and provided dividend payments of about $9 million per year.

Trying to climb upmarket

The company prides itself on following a strategy that has separated it from the less profitable and more volatile for-hire segments of the market. In the words of the company's 10-K: "The impact of this downturn on Covenant has been limited because we invested in M&A and growth assets in less cyclical end markets, primarily in our specialized Dedicated and Expedited units. (...) Although this business mix has lowered our downside risk during the extended downturn, it will also limit our flexibility to quickly reprice our services in the next strong freight market."

As part of this, the company has made several acquisitions in Dedicated (Landair in 2018, and LTST in 2023) and Expedited (AAT Carriers). We can observe a growth in intangibles from the premiums paid for these businesses, which are coincident with debt financing growing as well.

Unfortunately, I do not believe the businesses are particularly attractive, independent of the recent trucking downturn. As already commented for many other trucking companies, the operating return on total assets is the best measure, in my opinion, of a company's competitive moat in such a capital-intensive segment. In this respect, Covenant's historical performance, and even after its recent acquisitions, has been fairly bad.

To put it in context, an operating return on assets of 6% implies that the average asset inside of CVLG's balance sheet does not generate enough operating income to pay its cost in debt. The company's debt yields approximately between 5% and 6% according to the latest 10-Q.

A caveat is needed, however, and that is that about 10% of the company's assets (under Other Assets) are the participation in the leasing JV (called TEL), which adds income below the operating income line. But even in this case, the average return on assets by removing that 10% would increase by 50 to 60 bps alone.

The company does not report separate assets for each segment, so it is hard to calculate returns on capital for each of them separately.

Valuation is elevated

Despite the fact that CVLG's stock has grown fairly consistently for more than a decade, I have my doubts about the underlying quality of the business, and of its ability to generate returns on a long-term fundamental basis.

Of course, a recovery of the trucking cycle would most logically lead to the company generating higher earnings than today (the Dedicated segment generated operating margins of 8% in 2022 vs 4% today, and Expedited margins of 18% vs. 6% today). However, most of that is, in my opinion, already priced into the name.

Using the above average operating return on assets for the past decade, CVLG should generate about $60 million in operating income across the cycle (almost twice today's ~$35 million TTM), on a $1 billion asset basis. To this, we can add the leasing business generating approximately $20 million in net income for the company (based on the average reported in the FY24 10-K between the high point of 2022 and the low point in 2024).

This implies $80 million in operating and equity income, on a long-term cycle-average basis. From this, we have to remove about $14 million in today's interest costs (potentially lower if rates decrease over time). Then, taxes on the $45 million after operating income and interest (the $20 million from the leasing JV are already taxed), or ~$11 million.

The total post-tax income of the company should be about $55 million on average across the cycle. Compared to this, the name already trades at $650 million, or an earnings yield of 8.5%, which does not feel attractive, in my opinion, for a name with such low returns on assets.

Growing with a low operating return on assets is hard because growth requires investment, and that investment eats into the 'meager' return. Each 1 pp of asset growth (~$10 million), needed for 1 pp of operating income growth at the same operating return on assets, eats up 1.5% of earnings yield at the current market cap. That means it is better for shareholders to invest in buybacks or dividends, not growth.

From all of this, I consider CVLG, despite its stellar performance as a stock since the early 2010s, and especially since 2020, is not an attractive name today, and I initiate with a Hold.

data.title: Commodity Price Surges: Unique, But Linked
data.description:
Editor's Note: Originally published at tsi-blog.com on January 22, 2026

[This blog post is an excerpt from a recent commentary at www.speculative-investor.com]

Whether it is war or accidents at major mines or weather or trade disputes or political upheaval or power shortages, every large rise in the price of any commodity will have its own unique drivers.

However, there are periods when many commodities experience large price rises. Even though each of these price rises will have its own fundamental reasons and will seem special when viewed in isolation, a broader view will indicate that they are related.

One reason for the existence of periods during which many commodities experience large price rises, each for a seemingly unique reason, is that long periods of under-investment in commodity production due to low prices make the markets far more vulnerable to supply disruptions.

Consequently, an issue (for example, a drought, a mine collapse, a civil war) in one part of the world that during some periods would be taken in stride and result in a relatively small rise in the international price, can lead to a globally important shortage and a huge price rise.

A second reason is the macroeconomic/monetary backdrop and a feedback loop involving commodities and conflict. Monetary inflation eventually leads to economic hardship by raising the cost of living and getting in the way of economic progress.

As the inflation problem becomes more obvious, commodities are hoarded both for insurance and speculation purposes, putting additional upward pressure on prices throughout the economy and exacerbating the hardship.

This leads to conflict within countries and between countries. If the conflict involves countries that are important in the global supply chain for any commodity, which it often does, then the price of that commodity will rise in dramatic fashion.

The current situation in the tin market is a relevant example. Over the past year there have been supply disruptions due to decisions to curtail production in Myanmar, where 10%-15% of the world’s tin supply originates, and conflict between government and rebel forces in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), where about 6% of the world’s tin supply originates.

These supply issues have led to the price surge illustrated below. While a short-term price peak probably will be set soon, this market will remain acutely vulnerable to supply disruptions due to the combination of additional demand from the building of data centres and long-term under-investment in mining.

There have been several dramatic price rises in commodity markets over the past six months, and we expect to see many more over the next two years. Each will have its own unique drivers, but almost all of them will be related to the underlying influences outlined above.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Davenport Value & Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Q4 2025 Market Review

The Davenport Value & Income Fund (DVIPX) generated a 1.47% total return in the fourth quarter of 2025, which compared to the Russell 1000 Value ® Index's 3.81% return over the same time frame. For the year, the Fund generated a 13.69% total return, versus the index's 15.91% return. Important to note: The index contains some very fine businesses that don't exhibit prototypical "value and income" characteristics. (The largest weighting in the Russell 1000 Value, Google (GOOGL) parent Alphabet (GOOGL), trades near 28x earnings and has a dividend yield of less than 0.3%, while the fourth-largest index weighting, Amazon (AMZN), has never paid a dividend. Google alone fully explains the difference between Value & Income's 2025 performance and that of the index).

Fund Update

Contributors: Several financial holdings were among the Fund's best quarterly performers, led by Citigroup Inc. (C) at +16%. Banks tend to "borrow short and lend long," and therefore stand to benefit from lower short-term interest rates that lead to a steeper yield curve. As strong as the banks were, parcel delivery companies were even stronger, with FedEx (FDX) Corp (FDX) at +23% pacing the Fund for the quarter. FedEx "delivered" a sizable beat-and-raise quarterly performance, and higher contracted rates appear to be sticking, even as fuel prices have declined. With structural costs being eliminated via facility closures and headcount reductions, the businesses are generating significant operating leverage. Specific to FedEx, the pending spin of its Freight segment remains on track for mid-2026.

Detractors: Worst for the quarter was Alexandria Real Estate Inc. (ARE) at -39%, as the depth and duration of the valley for biotech real estate appeared to be expanding, and the company signaled a potential dividend cut, which is at odds with our Fund's objective. Subsequent to our sale, the company cut its dividend by 45%. We also sold HP Inc. (HPQ) at -16% on concerns that surging memory prices could pressure PC demand.

Fund Activity

We mentioned in last quarter's letter chipping star performer Oracle (ORCL) Corp (ORCL) multiple times. Shares rose another 10% in the opening days of the fourth quarter amid what appeared to be peak enthusiasm surrounding its data center growth prospects, and we fully exited the position in October. During our nearly three years of ownership, Oracle's stock "graduated" from value to growth – surging from about 16x earnings at the time of our purchase to more than 40x (a 24-year high) at the time of our sale. The sale proved timely, as shares subsequently slumped amid questions about capital needed to fund growth.

Top Ten Equity Holdings † as of December 31, 2025

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) 4.08
L3Harris Technologies, Inc. 3.65
Nextera Energy, Inc. 3.52
TE Connectivity PLC (TEL)** 3.31
Anheuser-Busch Inbev SA (BUD)** 3.24
Chevron Corp (CVX) 3.22
American Tower Corp (AMT) 3.16
Fairfax Financial Holdings Ltd (FFH)** 3.00
Becton Dickinson and Co (BDX) 2.94
JPMorgan Chase & Co (JPM) 2.90

† Holdings are subject to change without notice.

** Foreign Holding. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to risk.

We added global insurance broker Marsh (MMC) & McLennan Companies Inc. (MMC), as well as specialty chemicals manufacturer Eastman (EMN) Chemicals Co (EMN). Eastman appears to be trading near a trough multiple on cyclical trough earnings, with already-enacted cost saving programs likely to benefit results in 2026 and beyond. The company recently raised its dividend for a 16th consecutive year, with shares yielding more than 5% at the time of our purchase, paying us handsomely while we await end-market recovery. Marsh strikes us as a quintessentially good business trading at a reasonable valuation – a relatively rare combination with broad market indices trading near all-time highs. Buying Marsh while selling Oracle offers a glimpse into our consistent effort to “be greedy when others are fearful, and fearful when others are greedy.” Insurance brokerages connect buyers and sellers of insurance with each other, while taking no underwriting risk. Although recent results have been toward the lower-end of historic ranges (3-5% organic growth), valuation near 10-year lows seems to amply reflect recent performance. If the company returns toward the middle to upper end of its historic performance bogey, we believe valuation has room to improve. In the meantime, shares yield 2% (Marsh has raised its dividend 14 consecutive years), and the company’s strong balance sheet could enable it to accelerate its buyback pace, taking advantage of the stock’s discounted valuation.

Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD) reinstated its interim dividend, while McDonald’s Corp (MCD), Exxon Mobil Corp (XOM), Fidelity National Financial Inc. (FNF), and Becton-Dickinson and Co. (BDX) each continued their annual streak of dividend enhancements (at 49, 43, 10, and 54 years, respectively). In 2025, 36 of our Fund’s 42 holdings increased their dividends (by an average of 7% year-over-year), while 30 reduced their share count via buybacks (by 1.2% on average).

Conclusion

Given the market’s strong 2025 returns, it would be easy to forget a nearly 20% drawdown in the spring, a period during which Value & Income played good “defense” by holding up relatively well. That’s consistent with what we expect the Fund to do: Protect on the way down, participate on the way up. We believe a well-diversified, lower-volatility strategy such as Value & Income offers investors the opportunity to benefit from compounding by helping them stay invested through the market’s inevitable ups and downs.

Sincerely,

George L. Smith III, CFA®Co-manager, Value &Income Portfolio

Adam Bergman, CFA®Co-manager, Value &Income Portfolio

Michael S. Beall, CFA®Co-manager, Value &Income Portfolio

data.title: Micron Technology: Structural Demand Is Replacing Cyclical Volatility
data.description:
Introduction

The global semiconductor industry is currently navigating a large shift, transitioning from reliance on consumer-driven hardware cycles to structural demand driven by the intense requirements of AI infrastructure. Memory has taken a role where it is one of the central architects of computational performance. Big technology firms like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) are scrambling to get their hands on memory from suppliers like Micron Technology (MU).

I am initiating coverage on Micron with a Strong Buy rating. I believe MU is a compelling investment opportunity for those looking to capitalize on this market backdrop, with my main arguments being that MU presents a case where it is moving past historical cyclicality into a phase with structural demand, a strong competitive advantage over competitors in high-performance memory and its underlooked automotive segment, and an excellent growth story paired with improving margins and further strong revenue growth to come at an attractive valuation.

Background

The rapid proliferation of generative AI and large language models has resulted in massive amounts of capital being invested in AI infrastructure. According to Goldman Sachs, CapEx estimates for AI hyperscalers have risen to $527 billion in 2026 following guidance from Q3 earnings. Additionally, McKinsey estimates global data center capacity demand to increase by 3.5x from 2025 to 2030, reaching 156 gigawatts of demand reserved solely for AI workloads.

Furthermore, an estimated 60% to 80% of this CapEx goes towards purchasing GPUs, and 66% of the cost of GPUs is represented by HBM (high bandwidth memory) and advanced packaging. Using these numbers, we can arrive at a rough market size of memory in 2026. With a CapEx estimate of $527 billion in 2026, an assumption of 30% to 40% of CapEx being GPUs (I use a conservative estimate here to account for costs related to racks, networking, etc.), and 66% of GPU costs being memory, we arrive at a market of $104 billion to $140 billion in 2026 as shown in the chart above.

Another key dimension to consider when looking at the broader background of this investment thesis on MU is the technical necessity of high-performance memory. SemiAnalysis finds that the rapid advancements in logic chips have outpaced the ability of memory to feed them data, creating a "memory wall." The above chart from SemiAnalysis shows that compute performance increases from 21 TFLOPS to 10,000 TFLOPS (+475x), while memory bandwidth only increases from 732 GB/s to 8,000 GB/s (+11x) from 2016 to 2024. This imbalance shows that innovations in high-bandwidth memory are a structural requirement for modern AI accelerators.

Memory is No Longer a Cyclical Commodity

In the traditional memory demand cycle, demand was discretionary and consumer-facing. The memory industry's core demand drivers (smartphones, gaming consoles, PCs, etc.) were themselves cyclical - this is particularly evident when looking at the PlayStation release cycle shown in the image above, where console releases are spaced multiple years apart.

Additionally, within this traditional demand cycle, there was a lack of supply discipline where memory manufacturers would engage in aggressive capacity expansion. This was driven by the fact that there are very high fixed costs in developing fabrication plants, and so, with fully utilized fabs to take advantage of economies of scale, supply would flood the market, resulting in lowered chip prices leading to margin pressure driven by this cyclicality.

However, the present cycle is fundamentally different. The primary demand driver has shifted from consumer products to AI infrastructure, where AI servers and data centers are not discretionary purchases. AI workloads require high-bandwidth, low-latency memory that is not interchangeable with commodity DRAM and cannot be easily stockpiled due to the fact that HBM stacks are qualified per GPU generation and there are rapid transitions between technology, so older inventory faces obsolescence faster than in consumer products. Further decreasing cyclicality is NVDA's annual GPU release cadence.

On December 3, 2025, MU announced an exit from Crucial, its consumer-facing brand focused on providing RAM and SSDs for computers. According to Sumit Sadana, Chief Business Officer of Micron, the exit from Crucial is driven by AI-driven growth in the data center segment and an imperative to improve and support this segment. In my opinion, this exit further reinforces my thesis, wherein cyclicality is no longer a significant concern for a business like MU.

The Micron Competitive Advantage

Within the HBM market, MU has very rapidly caught up to the market leader, SK hynix Inc. (HXSC.F). In 2022, Micron's HBM market share was just 10%; by Q2 2025, Micron held a 21% market share, overtaking Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF) with its 17% market share and positioning itself as a serious challenger to SK Hynix at 62%. Although SK Hynix leads the market currently, Micron's HBM4 samples are hitting 11 Gbps pin speeds, surpassing the 10 Gbps rates of SK Hynix, suggesting to me that the gap is closing and MU is set to capture more market share in this premium, high-margin HBM segment.

On the DRAM front, Micron's 1-gamma DRAM node is the industry's first to reach mature yields, 50% faster than the previous generation. This DRAM node also presents a greater than 20% reduction in power density and greater than 30% bit density compared to previous-generation DRAM nodes. In contrast, it was reported that Samsung had struggled to commercially mass-produce DRAM faster than its competitors. Furthermore, MU is currently developing 1-delta and 1-epsilon nodes, which are expected to have further innovations.

Out of MU's four business units, I believe its Automotive and Embedded Business Unit (AEBU) is one that is generally underlooked by analysts, and it is an area of significant future demand. Although it is currently MU's smallest business unit by revenue, it has shown 48.5% year-on-year growth in Q3 2025 with a gross profit expansion of 125% since last year to 45%. The robotaxi market is still in its nascent stage but has potential for explosive growth. GOOG's Waymo plans to expand to 20 cities by the end of 2026, expecting to deliver 1 million robotaxi rides a week by the end of 2026. Additionally, McKinsey forecasts the autonomous driving sector to create $300 billion to $400 billion in revenue by 2035, with a breakdown shown above.

Within this specific domain, MU's competitive advantage arises from a comprehensive automotive product portfolio, with some of these key products being automotive-grade DRAM, SSDs, NOR flash, etc. Furthermore, today, the average vehicle utilizes approximately 90 GB of combined RAM and NAND, but in 2026, this requirement is expected to grow to 278 GB, reaching as high as 2 TB in high-end autonomous systems and 4 TB by 2030. MU estimates that fully autonomous vehicles need to support an aggregate memory bandwidth exceeding 1 terabit per second.

MU also leads on a technical front within the AEBU segment, with MU being the first and only player in the industry to achieve an ASIL-D (the highest of the Automotive Safety Integrity Levels) certification for its LPDDR5X DRAM. This LPDDR5X DRAM offers a 15% to 25% bandwidth increase over the previous generation, with 10% lower power consumption and 6% additional addressable memory space. I believe that MU's strategic focus on AEBU is reminiscent of NVDA's early identification of AI-oriented GPUs as a significant growth area and provides an edge over competitors not prioritizing this strategic area of growth as much as MU.

Financial Performance

In Q3 2025, MU reported revenue of $13.6 billion, representing a quarter-on-quarter increase of 20.6% and a year-on-year increase of 57%. Most of this growth is concentrated within their cloud memory segment (which includes revenue from HBM and DRAM). This revenue is driven by high levels of hyperscaler CapEx for AI training and inference. I expect this segment to continue growing with increasingly higher amounts of CapEx deployed towards AI infrastructure in the coming years, combined with MU's increasing prowess in HBM, as previously established.

What is even more impressive about MU's growth story is how they've managed to substantially increase net income margins over the last few quarters whilst also growing revenue. Above is a chart where Q1 2024 net income margin is indexed at 100 to show how net income margins have evolved since Q1 2024 for Samsung, Micron, and SK Hynix. MU shows an impressive index score of 788.71, representing an almost 8x increase to a 38.4% net income margin since Q1 2024.

Although this is impressive, SK Hynix has the highest raw net income margin at 51.5%, showing a growth of 3.3x. This signals to me there is still room for net income margin expansion for MU as well. Samsung has trailed behind MU and SK Hynix, although I will note that net income margin data for Samsung Electronics is clouded by the inclusion of non-memory segments since segmented financial data is not available.

Valuation

When looking at the forward multiples of MU, it is clear that the company is undervalued, with many forward multiples under the sector medians by a large margin, as shown above. Additionally, MU has an incredibly low forward PEG ratio of 0.22x, much lower than the sector median of 1.65x.

To value MU, I am going to use a forward P/E ratio of 22.09x - this is the average of its peer set of comparable public companies, shown above. This yields a price per share of $739.2, which is my 1-year price target for MU. I expect this premium over the current multiple to be driven by further margin improvements, market share expansion, and the market pricing in the fact that this is no longer as cyclical a business as it once was.

Risks

The largest risk to my thesis is competitive pressure from SK Hynix or Samsung - these companies have vast R&D and manufacturing capabilities. Both companies have heavily invested in increasing manufacturing capacity, which could lead to pricing pressure. Furthermore, innovations made by the aforementioned companies could also lead to their market share expansion. Additionally, Chinese firms are also making an effort to break into the memory market, potentially exacerbating pricing pressure.

Much of the current demand is driven by the AI infrastructure buildout by hyperscalers and neoclouds. It could be the case that further advancements in LLMs do not require this much CapEx, as was previously feared, with DeepSeek being a lower-cost model.

Conclusion

I believe that MU presents a very compelling investment opportunity given that MU's once consumer-driven cyclicality is replaced by structural demand from AI. MU presents a strong competitive advantage in high-performance memory for GPUs, and in its automotive product portfolio, there is room for margin expansion, and it is valued at a significantly discounted multiple with ample room for multiple expansion. The market backdrop is also favorable, with record amounts being invested into AI infrastructure CapEx. Furthermore, there is a significant technical need for HBM advancements in developing more advanced GPUs.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Upwork: A 'Strong Buy' With Aggressive Targets, New AI Offerings (Upgrade)
data.description:
Though the stock market has kicked off in a volatile manner so far in 2026, investors are still latching onto the AI theme and trying to determine which companies will be winners and which will be losers. A massive technological shift is happening at the moment, and investors who can spot companies at the beginning of a major business transformation can realize significant gains.

Upwork (UPWK), in my view, is doing a terrific job at crystallizing its vision for how freelance and contingent work will cooperate with AI. The work platform has seen its share price rise ~20% over the past year, slightly outperforming the S&P 500. In my view, the stock still sits at low multiples that are ripe for further upward re-rating.

I last wrote a "Buy" rating on Upwork in November, when the stock was trading near $17 per share. Since then, Upwork has continued to rally higher, especially after the company's recent investor day update, which unveiled aggressive new targets for FY28. Against the company's reinvigorated growth expectations, I continue to think that Upwork's valuation multiples underappreciate the company's ability to take share in the contingent workforce, especially with many companies looking for quick expertise in AI integration. I'm upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy."

FY28 plan calls for mid-teens revenue, >20% adjusted EBITDA CAGR

Let's begin the discussion by recapping the "new news" that emerged out of Upwork's investor day in late November, which tacked on new information and new targets on top of the company's most recent quarterly update. The biggest headline that investors shouldn't miss is the fact that the company outlined a new medium-term operating model. Its three-year outlook through FY28 calls for 13%-15% revenue growth through the next three years, 7%-9% growth in GSV (total spend on the platform, regardless of Upwork's cut), and ~20% growth in adjusted EBITDA.

As a reminder, for FY25, Upwork has guided to a midpoint of $785.5 million in revenue and $223.5 million in adjusted EBITDA:

Applying a revenue and EBITDA CAGR of 14% and 20%, respectively, gives us FY28 revenue of $1.16 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $331.1 million (29% margin) - a meaningful scale for a company that many consider to be a niche solution.

Clearer segmentation between enterprise and SMB offerings in a >$1 trillion market

Of course, it perhaps doesn't need to be stated that any company can paint aggressive targets and hope that the market believes it can achieve them. What I like about Upwork, however, is that in its investor day, the company has more clearly defined how it plans to grab more market share in its massive TAM.

Upwork's core thesis is that AI is "fractionalizing work." What this means is that work streams are now becoming more and more specialized (for example, instead of hiring generalist product marketing talent, companies may seek a dedicated SEO specialist for optimizing text on a Shopify storefront). This has created an opportunity for highly specialist experts, armed with AI, to provide consultative services to businesses of various sizes. Effectively, Upwork imagines a future of work in which there are more proverbial cooks in the kitchen, each great at a specific niche.

Upwork has always distinguished itself by making it easy to hire and manage contract labor, but it's doing a better job now at delineating how to segregate its services for the enterprise versus the SMB markets. For the large enterprise space, Upwork continues to double down on its contingent workforce management tool, Lifted.

As shown in the snapshot above, Lifted is both a talent-sourcing platform and an organizational management software platform that manages the onboarding and offboarding of contract labor and the projects they have in their pipeline.

The company is driving real results in this space. Lifted is driving meaningful GSV growth, while Upwork itself is integrating AI into its work streams and reducing its headcount by over 20%. Per CEO Hayden Brown's remarks on the investor day:

We also realized in the Enterprise, we could go further and compete better and bigger. So we made our biggest moves in the company's history on Enterprise, buying 2 companies this past year, relaunching our Enterprise business as lifted in August. And the reception has been amazing. We've already heard from so many customers who are excited to use us in new and different ways and inviting us for bigger and better RFPs than what we were able to compete with before. The strategy is clearly working. We're seeing Business Plus, it's grown 33% on GSV quarter-on-quarter in Q3. So it's already got tremendous traction.

This was also not enough though, we also wanted to go further. We reinvented our operational layer, fully went deep. We reduced our team size in 2024 by 21%.

In the SMB space, meanwhile, Upwork is armed with a different set of offerings. Its core product for SMBs is Uma, which helps smaller businesses draft job posts and manage the application process for contract labor.

There is a large gap here because many SMBs want to leverage the possibilities provided by AI and automation but lack the technical know-how within their small workforces to implement it. This is where Uma fills that gap: giving small businesses easy access to AI expertise at reasonable prices. Uma Recruiting enables rapid hiring, alongside flexible net-30 payment terms that can give payment flexibility to perennially cash-strapped SMBs. The results are evident, with the company noting that its Business Plus segment saw 33% sequential growth in GSV.

With clear segmentation and a clear message to both investors and customers on how Upwork operates alongside AI (and is not being disrupted by AI), we note that Upwork has repositioned its 2028 TAM at a staggering $1.3 trillion in GSV - of which $530 billion sits in SMBs, and $650 billion in enterprise.

Upwork's current ~$4 billion GSV trend is less than 0.5% penetrated into this overall market. Though we continue to expect that contract labor will be a highly fragmented market, we think Upwork's cohesive AI strategy and clearer enterprise/SMB segmentation will help gain the company a stronger foothold in this massive industry.

Q4 look-ahead and valuation

Upwork is next expected to report Q4 results in early/mid-February. To me, these are the core items to watch:

  • Growth acceleration in both SMB and enterprise segments. Upwork is benefiting from both rapid startup creation (thanks to AI enabling rapid product development) and new products like Uma and Lifted targeted toward SMBs and enterprises, respectively. We expect the sales push behind these two products to drive acceleration in Upwork's top-line GSV and revenue growth.
  • FY26 targets ahead of CAGR expectations. Alongside Q4 results, Upwork is also expected to provide a first-look guidance at FY26. We also expect FY26 top-line growth to accelerate versus FY25, given a full year in the market for Lifted. We think that Upwork setting growth targets ahead of its FY28 CAGR expectations will drive further optimism for the stock.
  • Evidence of continued internal AI optimization and efficiency. Upwork has recently been minting a ~30% adjusted EBITDA margin thanks to its commitments to headcount reductions. We're also on the lookout for the company to announce more internal usage of AI to drive further economies of scale.

Despite its myriad advantages, we continue to find Upwork to trade at a very reasonable valuation. At current share prices just shy of $20, Upwork trades at a market cap of $2.57 billion. Netting off the $643.1 million of cash and $359.3 million of debt on Upwork's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $2.29 billion.

If we assume a ~20% adjusted EBITDA growth target for FY26 (in line with the company's FY28 growth CAGR for adjusted EBITDA), FY26 adjusted EBITDA would be $268.2 million. This positions Upwork's valuation at 8.5x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, Upwork's relatively cheap valuation is set against a number of operating risks as well. For one, there's the macroeconomy. Consumer spending is still weak, which may impact Upwork's core SMB market especially and reduce the demand for contingent/contract labor, as companies look to first protect jobs for their own employees.

Second, with the increasing proliferation of no/low-code AI tools, many smaller businesses may decide to take a "DIY" approach rather than utilize Uma to find specialists to help them integrate AI for their own needs. If Upwork can't demonstrate the value of its services in this new "fractionalized work" environment, and if small businesses get comfortable enough using AI tools on their own, the company's growth thesis may not play out perfectly.

That said, we think the company's recent spate of product rollouts, its ambitious FY28 growth plans, and its cohesive message on how Upwork delivers AI results will all be tailwinds to this stock, especially at such a modest valuation. Stay long here and buy ahead of earnings.

data.title: Oklo: Meta Just Turned A Maybe Into A When
data.description:
In my last coverage on Oklo Inc. (OKLO), I maintained a hold rating, arguing that valuation was far ahead of fundamentals and warning that there are no near term news or events to keep the stock afloat.

I was dead wrong (well, partially, given that shares are down double digits since my last coverage).

On January 9, the company put out a press release, stating that Meta agreed to advance Oklo's Pike County 1.2 GW campus. In my view, this is a major development, as it is a clear external validation for the tech behind the Aurora powerhouse. On top of that, the deal adds a way for Meta to prepay for power, which will help fund fuel procurement and early Phase 1 work.

All this lands in a year when the Street expects another round zero in sales. Even though Oklo is not out of the woods from a dilution perspective, despite its $1.18B liquidity, I believe this stock is the best positioned in the advanced nuclear reactor space. On licensing, the stock needs visible progress in the COLA submission, which was initially planned for 2025. I believe this will be a key topic in the next earnings release.

Overall, I upgrade to a buy after the Meta deal, although the high beta could pressure the stock in the near term.

The Meta Deal

On January 9, Meta announced it will advance Oklo's Pike County 1.2 GW campus to support Meta's data centers in the region, specifically naming the new Prometheus supercluster.

In my view, this is a significant step in validating the technology behind the Aurora powerhouse.

From a timeline perspective, I wouldn't hold my breath. The first phase is targeted to come online "as early as 2030". Oklo doesn't expect the full 1.2 GW to be deployed until 2034. Yet, despite this fact, shares jumped by double digits on the announcement. Why?

Well, I believe there are many reasons. The first one is that the agreement with Meta provides a mechanism to prepay for power. This is key, given that the Street expects 2026 to be another dry year from a sales perspective. According to PR, Oklo will use the funds to secure nuclear fuel and advance Phase 1, including early procurement and development activities.

Unfortunately, the company didn't disclose the actual dollar amount. All we have is a "multi-billion-dollar investment over the long-term clean energy agreement".

From a consumer perspective (a hot topic given Trump's affordability agenda heading into the midterm elections), the deal makes sense as Meta will be paying the full costs for the energy used. So, even though the additional power from the Aurora powerhouses will be pumped into PJM, Meta will absorb the full energy costs (without passing the burden to Ohio locals, in theory).

Finally, I strongly believe that Oklo rallied after the announcement, as the Meta deal is a strong validation of the company's nuclear technology. That deserved an instant re-rate, even though shares are now down to below pre-announcement levels.

Looking ahead, there are a few milestones to keep an eye on this year. The pre-construction and site characterization of the Pike County 1.2 GW campus is scheduled for 2026. I believe this will be a hot topic in the next earnings season, especially during the Q&A, as analysts are likely to ask details on the size, timing, and conditions of the Meta deal (even though I don't expect massive disclosures beyond the recent press release).

Financial Snapshot And Q4 Developments

The company exited Q3 2025 with $410M in cash and equivalents. On top of that, the company has $511.6M in current debt securities and another $262M in non-current debt securities.

This brings the total liquidity of the company to a decent $1.18B

A quick look at the cash flow statement shows $48.7M in net cash used in operations for the first 9-months of 2025. Annualized, I obtain a cash burn of $65M.

A naive cash runway estimation (utilizing the $65M annual cash burn) yields 18 years. Of course, the cash burn is likely to increase significantly in the next few years as they develop the Aurora powerhouses for the 1.2 GW Ohio campus. Given how new this technology is, no one knows the exact costs. As I (almost never) say, it's better safe than sorry, so the company announced a new $1.5B ATM program in December. Interestingly enough, Oklo was up over 15% on that day, which means that investors are welcoming the new dilution. Why?

Because costs are likely to skyrocket from here. The key items that I would closely monitor with a company like Oklo are the cash burn and the R&D costs. Speaking of which, the Q3 income statement shows a 196% (call it 200%) increase yoy in the last quarter.

Overall, I'm not worried by liquidity at the moment.

Risks

This is a zero-revenue company, and the Street doesn't project a positive EPS until 2030.

Call me crazy, but I don't tend to rely on such long-term projections, as the world is quite volatile.

Until then, I expect dilution to increase the number of outstanding shares at a similar historical rate.

Even though the market didn't react negatively to the new $1.5B ATM program announced in December, I think downside risk is ahead once the company starts leaning on it to raise equity capital.

Other risks include the broader selloff in the AI trade, especially since October last year. A large part of the power narrative is tied to AI data centers, and Meta has been in the spotlight since its last earnings release. Overall, I'm not worried about Meta's overspending commitment to AI data centers, but the low sentiment around Meta's stock could spill over into Oklo, given the recent partnership.

Another risk comes from a regulatory perspective, which I will discuss below.

Valuation

Oklo has no sales, no earnings, and no EBITDA. I strongly doubt that investors are looking at valuation right now. The few that do have one key metric: its price-to-book value.

The stock is trading at 16x its forward book value. Is that a lot? Well, that's 780% above the utilities sector median. Compared to NuScale's 5.5x multiple, Oklo seems to be highly overpriced.

Bear in mind that NuScale is way ahead of Oklo in the NRC design certification process. NuScale obtained its Part 52 design certification back in 2023, and the NRC issued a Standard Design Approval for the NuScale US460 design in May 2025.

That said, even though NuScale is ahead with the design certification, that approval is not the same as a license to build a specific plant at a specific site. That would need a Combined License, and here is where I see Oklo taking the lead. The company completed a pre-application readiness assessment for Phase 1 in July 2025, and the NRC said it found no significant gaps that would hinder application acceptance. Looking at the upcoming Q4 earnings, I would closely monitor any updates from the pre-application to the actual COLA submissions. According to the July PR, the company planned to submit the first phase of the Aurora-INL COLA this year. From what I'm seeing on NRC's public Oklo project page, they haven't submitted it yet.

Conclusion

Like it or not, the share price is currently moving with the tide of other advanced reactor stocks. You can clearly see the high beta in the chart below.

At the same time, the broader advanced nuclear reactor segment is moving with the sentiment in the AI space. Notice that the peak in the charts above was around mid-end October. That was the last time the XLK saw new highs.

Excluding the high beta of these stocks, I believe Oklo is well-positioned, at least from a cash perspective. If I add up the new $1.5B ATM program to the Q3 liquidity, I obtain $2.68B in cash, equivalents, and short- and long-term investments. Not bad. That should give them enough fuel to advance the Pike County 1.2 GW campus, with the first phase planned "as early as 2030".

data.title: Looking Ahead At What 2026 May Hold For Union Pacific
data.description:
Introduction

Railroads are often considered to be one of the best real-time indicators of national economic health for fairly obvious reasons--we don't have to wait for years to see how much stuff is being moved around the U.S. in bulk, which we can translate, broadly, to economic activity.

When things are stable, businesses like Union Pacific Corporation (UNP) are difficult to beat--they command solid incremental margins and have a firm grip on pricing power.

On the flip side, unstable times can generate periods of serious concern for management and investors alike. After all, as powerful as the railroads may be, they cannot generate demand for the products they ship. In light of the current uncertainty that seems to be emerging as the theme for 2026, we think that this predictable, stable business might deserve a little more interest from investors than it might normally warrant.

Let's dive in.

The Business, Valuation, & Efficiency

Let's start with some of the basic facts around Union Pacific. Per the company's last 10-K, the company operates 32,880 route miles across the country, which includes (importantly) Mexican and Canadian gateways. The company generated roughly $23 billion in revenues in 2024, stemming from three commodity groups: Bulk (grain, fertilizer, food, etc.), Industrial (chemicals, forest products, metals, oil & gas), and Premium (finished automobiles). Importantly, the business has largely been evenly spread across the three segments.

Turning to how the market values Union Pacific, the picture is relatively unsurprising for a company as, well, predictable (dare we say boring?) as UNP.

Over the last 10 years, the market has assigned an average forward PE and EV/EBITDA of 19x and 12.6x, respectively. Neither of these historic numbers is far off from where the stock sits today with a PE of 19x forward earnings estimate and an EV/EBITDA of 13x.

Union Pacific also has what we would consider to be a solid, dependable management team with exceptionally long tenures among C-suite executives (CEO James Vena has been in his position for a little more than 2 years, we should note).

As a bit of support for that claim, we note that over the last 8 quarters, Union Pacific has met its expected top line revenues only twice (it has missed by slim margins for the last 6 quarters), but the company's EBIT results have been flipped, exceeding analyst estimates 6 of the last 8 quarters.

Importantly, GAAP EPS results have also tracked positively for 6 out of 8 quarters. We think that this reflects management's ability to do more with what they can directly control in terms of cost and thus deliver positively for shareholders despite some suffering top line results.

What to Expect for Q1 and 2026 as a Whole

Given the wild start that 2026 has had and the ever-more-frequent collision of geopolitics with financial markets and economies on a global scale, it would seem foolish to make bold predictions about what this year will bring, so we'll tread lightly on assumptions and instead game out some scenarios for Union Pacific.

This month the World Bank published its outlook that global economic growth will slow overall from 2.7% to 2.6% while leaving in a bit of an asterisk that tariffs remain something of an X factor in predicting how the year will turn out.

Goldman Sachs (GS), meanwhile, published a rosy outlook for the U.S. in 2026, predicting that "tax cuts, real wage gains, and rising wealth should sustain solid consumer spending growth".

Let's consider what would be a bull case for Union Pacific in 2026--inflation and tariff headwinds could be a real boon for the stock over the coming year, which probably comes as a surprise to few. Increased economic capacity generally means more goods movement, more industrial production, and inventory purchases, much of which will need to be supported by railroad freight. Nationwide CapEx spending could also receive a boost from even hints that the economy is remaining more resilient than expected, which also has the ability to meaningfully translate to better top line results for Union Pacific.

The bear case would involve a bit of sobering reality--2.8% GDP growth isn't exactly booming, after all. Indeed, 2.8% growth and its subsequent translation to higher freight volumes and business for Union Pacific seem like less of a bull case and more of a perfection case. In other words, bears might be inclined to view the projected 2.8% as the base-to-slightly-bearish case, given that any range of unpredictable things in the realm of geopolitics could affect the outcome for 2026.

So--what's an investor to do?

We think that when it comes to Union Pacific, management guidance will be key. Union Pacific management has proved to be relatively accurate in their guidance on earnings calls over the last 8 quarters or so, if not slightly conservative. Additionally, the theme of the company's conference calls of late has been overtly centered on efficiency:

  1. In the 3rd Quarter of 2025, Vena noted that "we set best-ever quarterly records in workforce productivity, fuel consumption, terminal dwell, and train length."
  2. In the Q2 2025 earnings call, 'efficient' and 'efficiency' were mentioned 23 times.

What we are looking out for in the next earnings call is anything that deviates from the message of efficiency--which we read as another way to highlight corporate belt-tightening--to a message of volume. If this happens (and again, it is likely to be subtle in this upcoming conference call if it's there at all), then it could portend a slight re-rating of Union Pacific to the upside.

The logic for this is simple--companies in CapEx heavy industries (like railroads) are slow to expand capacity, especially in lean times. When demand surges, these companies' ability to control price is unparalleled.

What About the Merger?

Of course, we would be remiss if we didn't at least mention the looming and possibly not happening merger between Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern (NSC). While the details of the deal are outside the scope of our article today, we do note that the Transportation Surface Board recently kicked back Union Pacific's proposal, citing that it "lacked required information." For those looking for greater insight on this particular aspect of Union Pacific's current outlook, we recommend two great pieces by Seeking Alpha authors that present opposing cases (the bull case here by Blue Chip Portfolios and a more neutral case here by Patrick Doyle).

Risks

The risks we see to Union Pacific's outlook are myriad, but none of them pose an extreme threat to the stock's current valuation in our view. Geopolitical risks remain top of mind, as the current regime of tariffs has proved unpredictable in both implementation and in economic fallout with consumers. Doom-and-gloom expectations have so far proved inaccurate, but at the same time, it's difficult to ignore that the U.S. consumer is in a precarious place generally. That being said, we see more upside to Union Pacific's outlook than downside overall.

The Bottom Line

Conservative, mature industries like railroads don't often excite or move investors in the same way as tech stocks might, but even in these industries meaningful re-ratings are not off the board. Union Pacific has spent the last few years staring down headwinds of inflation and economic uncertainty. This year we'll be looking for signs that Union Pacific's days of belt-tightening may be ending, with a long-term view towards a re-rating of the stock to the upside.
